author: @MrSmokinMirrors
text: Are you sure you are using the correct terminology? Graphene is specifically graphite that is 1 atom thick. I highly doubt that if you mixed that much concrete with only a 1 atom thick level of graphite - that you would get ANY improvement. Are you sure you don't mean Graphite? I'm thinking you are mixing a certain amount of concrete with a much more amount of carbon than just 1 atom thick carbon. Since you haven't revealed the percentages of each - I can't say whether you're correct or not.

author: @yodaiam1000
text:
As an engineer, I would like to see testing on creep of the concrete. If the creep is drastically reduced, we could make slabs of concrete thinner. Creep is typically the biggest factor in determining the amount of deflection in a slab or beam. Thinner slabs means lighter buildings which makes the seismic design etc easier and cheaper. I know that graphene is great for tension but if the graphene fibers could help reduce the compression in the cement within the concrete mix, then it might help with creep.

It will be interesting to see the tensile capacity as well. If you can get consistent results above 20 MPa, that would be interesting and would have significant implications (assuming the cost of the concrete doesn't skyrocket). This means the possibility of concrete without rebar and that changes the very way we design and build. It makes 3D printing a building or building components a more practical option. Extruding concrete structural elements also becomes more practical. However, a 20 MPa tensile stress is a tall order to fill.

I am also curious about the slump and cement content. If you get increased flow with the graphene, this can reduce the cement ratio. This reduces the amount of cement production. Approximately 7% of human caused CO2 comes from cement production.

replies.author: @PetraKann
replies.text:
Good point, re creep characteristics. 

Should to compare this modified graphene concrete with new geopolymers that are coming onto the market. graphene is still very expensive per kg. Also compare with glass fibre reinforced concrete

author: @AaronHarper
text:
Brilliant Tony, well done.  Awesome job in showing the real world results, after all, that is where this stuff will be used.  We haven't done that test yet, but we'll let you know what we find.

So far you win...  we are at 51n using bog standard concrete, sand treated with GO, graphene added to the mix, and 28 day cure. Others show even more promise at 7 days (33n), but we'll see next month for the full cure. Sometimes the two cure figures don't track like we think they should.  Keep that tenner ready Rob! :)

replies.author: @spankeyfish
replies.text:
MrArcher0+ Caution is appropriate. I visited an RAF firefighting unit in the 90s and they always went in full breathing gear when dealing with aircraft fires involving carbon fibre. Nobody knew what the health effects were but carbon fibre produces microscopic shards when it's burnt that look just like asbestos under a microscope.
Materials often have different properties on the nanoscale; you call lick a lump of quartz all day and all you'll do is wear your tongue down but grind it into nanoparticles and breathe it and you'll get silicosis.

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: If miners suffered from 'miner's lung', caused by inhalation of coal dust, then why should carbon fibres or nanotubes behave any differently in the lung? Nobody can be sure until any health issues begin to come to light, perhaps up to forty years down the road. It would be wise to assume the worst, and to take precautions, until fears are shown to be unfounded.

author: @smasimulationshop1028
text:
Nice work guys!
Please check PH level changes as concrete is also a chemical protection for steel rebars. 
Also, please let us know about the strength of the concrete after 28 days, although that 7 days result is just amazing.
Finally, please consider start doing the test with a high strength concrete. Starting from let's say 50N/mm2 and adding graphene may be quite interesting to get a super concrete.
Awesome work.

replies.author: @tonyfreeman3800
replies.text: Thank you. When I make the next batch I will take note of the PH levels. The point of the project is to use basic materials and make the concrete super strong however we are planning on using different strengths to compare properties. Watch this space!
